data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-05T01:37:44.711Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating a .NET 10 web API to .NET 11. The app uses EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider.
We have a repository pattern where some methods use synchronous LINQ:

```csharp
public class ProductRepository
{
    private readonly CosmosDbContext _context;

    public List<Product> GetAll()
    {
        return _context.Products.ToList();
    }

    public Product GetById(int id)
    {
        return _context.Products.First(p => p.Id == id);
    }

    public int GetCount()
    {
        return _context.Products.Count();
    }

    public void Save(Product product)
    {
        _context.Products.Add(product);
        _context.SaveChanges();
    }
}
```

In .NET 10 we had the sync-over-async opt-in configured. What breaks in .NET 11?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about what breaks when migrating from .NET 10 to .NET 11 with EF Core using the Azure Cosmos DB provider, specifically regarding synchronous LINQ operations.

This is about a known breaking change in EF Core. Let me recall what I know:

In EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider, synchronous operations were never natively supported - they were implemented as sync-over-async internally. In EF Core 10 (.NET 10), there was an opt-in mechanism to allow this pattern. In .NET 11 (EF Core 11), this sync-over-async support is being removed entirely.


The Cosmos DB provider's underlying SDK is fundamentally async-only, so EF Core was previously bridging that gap by blocking on async calls. This approach is problematic because it can cause deadlocks and thread pool starvation, which is why the team is removing this compatibility layer in the next version.

I'm confident about the core issue: sync methods like `ToList()`, `SaveChanges()`, and `First()` will throw an `InvalidOperationException` instead of silently working through sync-over-async. The solution is straightforward—switch to the async equivalents like `ToListAsync()`, `SaveChangesAsync()`, and `FirstAsync()`.
